annotations.text: The hypokalemia reflected the magnitude of this excess cortisol.
Thus, I was left with a probable diagnosis of chronic hypertension.
the presence of hypokalemia increased my suspicion that the problem was secondary hypertension.

In Cushing's syndrome caused by excess corticotropin from any source, corticotropin levels are typically in the normal range — which is inappropriate given the level of cortisol — or they are elevated.
Low-dose dexamethasone suppression testing can be used to diagnose Cushing's syndrome despite the occurrence of false negative results.
The single finding that appropriately prompted an evaluation of this patient for Cushing's syndrome was unexplained hypokalemia.
The mass is not bright on T    -weighted imag

Nine histologic features are used to distinguish carcinoma from adenoma: a high mitotic rate (>5 per 50 high-power fields), atypical mitotic cells, venous invasion, and high nuclear grade (Fuhrman grade 3 to 4 on a scale from 1 to 4, with grade 1 indicating the least atypia and grade 4 the most),
the corticotropin level of 3 pg per milliliter (0.6 nmol per liter) in the setting of profound hypercortisolemia was strongly suggestive of an adrenal source of Cushing's syndrome,
The results of additional laboratory tests led me to suspect that she had Cushing's syndrome and to request a consultation with an endocrinologist.
